seoTitle: How to Record Sales Receipts Manually in QuickBooks Online
metaDescription: Manually enter sales receipts in QuickBooks Online for cash transactions and day-to-day sales that need accurate, immediate recording.
intro: Manually recording sales receipts is important for handling cash transactions and day-to-day sales that need to be captured accurately without relying on automated integrations. This guide walks through entering a sales receipt manually in QuickBooks Online.

steps.heading: Navigate to the sales receipt feature
steps.body: Open the section of QuickBooks Online dedicated to creating a new sales receipt.

steps.heading: Enter customer and transaction details
steps.body: Add the customer information along with the products or services sold and their amounts.

steps.heading: Select the payment method
steps.body: Specify how the payment was received, such as cash or card, so the transaction is categorized correctly.

steps.heading: Review the entry for accuracy
steps.body: Double-check that the amounts, customer, and payment method are all correctly recorded before saving.

steps.heading: Save the sales receipt
steps.body: Save the transaction so it's reflected in your income records and available for reporting.

faq.question: When should I use a sales receipt instead of an invoice?
faq.answer: Sales receipts are used when payment is received immediately, while invoices are for transactions where payment is expected later.

faq.question: Can manually recorded sales receipts integrate with point-of-sale systems?
faq.answer: Depending on your setup, QuickBooks Online can integrate with point-of-sale systems, though manual entry is still useful for standalone cash transactions.

faq.question: Is manual sales receipt entry suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, the process is straightforward and designed to be accessible for beginners while still supporting more advanced daily operations.

faq.question: What are common use cases for manual sales receipt entry?
faq.answer: Common use cases include cash sales, in-person transactions, and situations where automated integrations aren't available.
